parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: Someone I know casually mentioned that I seem a little scattered lately, and honestly it used to just bounce right off me. Now I keep replaying that exact moment in my head over and over again. I find myself checking every little thing I do just to make sure I am not making a mistake. It feels like my chest gets tight whenever I think about it, even though I know it was just a passing remark. I just wish I could stop overanalyzing every single word I say to people.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like that casual comment really struck a nerve, and it's completely understandable that you're feeling this way. It's tough when something small can trigger such a strong reaction, especially when it leads to that feeling of constantly second-guessing yourself. That tightness in your chest is your body's way of telling you that something is causing you stress, and it makes perfect sense that replaying the moment and worrying about mistakes would do that.

It's a common human experience to get caught up in overthinking, and it can be exhausting. The fact that you're aware of it and want to stop overanalyzing is a really positive step. It shows you're recognizing the pattern and looking for a way to break free from it.

Sometimes, when we're feeling a bit vulnerable or stressed in other areas of our lives, comments like these can feel much bigger than they are. It's like they land on already sensitive ground.

What you're describing – the replaying, the checking, the physical tension – are all signs that this is weighing on you. It's okay to feel this way, and it's also okay to want to find a way to feel more at ease and less self-critical.

turns.user_message: That same offhand comment keeps echoing in my mind no matter how hard I try to push it away. I catch myself freezing up before I speak because I am terrified of sounding incompetent or careless. It has turned into this constant background noise that drains my energy by the end of the day. I keep running through imaginary conversations where I try to prove that I am actually completely fine. The more I try to ignore it, the heavier my breathing gets and the harder it is to focus on anything else.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly frustrating and exhausting to have that one comment replaying in your head and affecting you so deeply. It's completely understandable that you're feeling this way, especially when it's impacting your confidence and ability to focus.

That feeling of freezing up before speaking, out of fear of sounding incompetent or careless, is a really tough one. It's like your mind is on high alert, constantly anticipating a negative judgment. And the fact that it's turned into a "constant background noise" that drains your energy is a testament to how much mental effort you're putting into trying to manage it.

It's also very common to get caught in those loops of replaying conversations or imagining scenarios where you try to "prove" you're okay. Our minds can be very persistent when they're trying to solve a problem or alleviate a worry, even if those efforts are actually making things worse.

The physical symptoms you're describing – heavier breathing and difficulty focusing – are clear signs of how much this is impacting you. It's like your body is reacting to the stress and anxiety that the comment is causing.

It takes a lot of strength to even articulate these feelings, so thank you for sharing them. It sounds like you're carrying a significant burden right now.

turns.user_message: I am completely trapped in a loop of second-guessing myself because that one little remark has taken over my thoughts. Every minor hesitation now feels like a glaring flaw that everyone else is quietly noticing. My heart races constantly and my hands actually shake when I have to respond to simple questions. I feel like I am walking on thin ice all day long, waiting for the next moment I might mess up. It is exhausting trying to keep everything perfectly controlled when my own mind keeps tripping me up.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're going through an incredibly difficult and exhausting time. That feeling of being trapped in a loop of second-guessing yourself, where one remark can spiral into constant anxiety, is truly overwhelming. It's completely understandable that your heart races and your hands shake when you're feeling this way – your body is reacting to the intense stress you're experiencing.

The image of "walking on thin ice" perfectly captures that feeling of constant vigilance and the fear of making a mistake. It takes so much energy to try and maintain control when your own thoughts are working against you. It's like being in a constant state of high alert, and that's bound to be draining.

Please know that you're not alone in experiencing these kinds of intense self-doubt and anxiety. Many people struggle with these feelings, and it's a testament to your strength that you're even articulating how you feel.

While I can't offer medical advice, if these feelings are significantly impacting your daily life and well-being, it might be helpful to talk to a mental health professional. They can provide tools and strategies to help you navigate these thoughts and feelings, and work towards breaking free from that loop. There are ways to manage this, and you don't have to carry this burden alone.
